messages.content: Lauren wants to clear out all the PDF school assignments currently stored in Dashboard/More/School/Approved Lists. She has a new list to upload and parse for JP and Joseph. Can you empty that folder or section so she can upload fresh?

Symptom: Lauren wants to clear out all existing PDF school assignments from Dashboard/More/School/Approved Lists to make room for uploading and parsing new assignments for JP and Joseph.

Likely cause: The school PDF storage and management system lives in `school_pdf_engine.py` which handles PDF parsing, storage, and retrieval for school assignments. The existing PDFs are likely stored in a specific directory or data structure that needs to be cleared, and there may be a function or route in `app.py` that handles the clearing operation for the approved lists section.

Risk areas: Clearing the wrong data could remove other important school records or curriculum data. The PDF parsing logic and file path management could break if the clearing operation doesn't properly reset associated metadata or index files. Other school-related routes in the app.py elif chain could be affected if they depend on the existing PDF structure.

Replit prompt:
Examine the school PDF system in school_pdf_engine.py and identify how approved school assignment PDFs are stored and managed. Create or modify a function to clear all existing PDF assignments from the Dashboard/More/School/Approved Lists section, ensuring that any associated metadata, index files, or tracking data is also properly reset. Add a route in app.py to handle a POST request for clearing approved lists (something like /clear-approved-pdfs) that calls this clearing function and returns a success confirmation. Follow Python 3.11 constraints: no backslashes inside f-strings and keep all imports at the top of files. Do not change any file other than school_pdf_engine.py and app.py unless absolutely necessary. Preserve all existing elif routing structure in do_POST and do_GET — do not convert any elif chain into separate if blocks and do not reorder existing elif branches. After making changes, confirm nothing in do_POST or do_GET was restructured.

Lauren's POD doesn't have a visible link to her Programs page, even though programs are being saved. Can you add a "View Your Programs" link in the sidebar or near the top of each person's POD so they can actually access saved workouts?

Symptom: Lauren's POD (and other family members' PODs) don't have a visible link to access the Programs page, even though programs are being saved successfully.

Likely cause: The POD rendering happens in `render_schedule.py` which generates the /today HTML for each person, and it likely doesn't include a "View Your Programs" link in the sidebar or navigation area. The programs functionality exists (in `render_programs.py`) but there's no UI link connecting the daily schedule view to the programs page.

Risk areas: Adding navigation links could interfere with the existing POD layout structure or sidebar rendering. The top_nav() function in ui_helpers.py might also need coordination to avoid duplicate or conflicting navigation elements. Changes to the POD template structure could affect mobile responsiveness or existing CSS styling.

Replit prompt:
In render_schedule.py, locate where the POD (daily schedule) HTML is generated for each person and add a "View Your Programs" link that navigates to /programs. Place this link prominently in the sidebar or near the top of the POD layout so it's easily accessible. The link should be visible for all family members, not just Lauren. Ensure the link styling matches the existing POD design patterns and doesn't disrupt the current layout. Follow Python 3.11 constraints: no backslashes inside f-strings and keep all imports at the top of the file. Do not change any file other than render_schedule.py unless absolutely necessary. Preserve all existing elif routing structure in do_POST and do_GET — do not convert any elif chain into separate if blocks and do not reorder existing elif branches. After making changes, confirm nothing in do_POST or do_GET was restructured.

Lauren needs a visible link on her POD that takes her directly to her saved exercise program ("6-Week Postpartum Core & Resilience Block"). Can you add a "View Your Program" or "Full Program Details" link in the exercise block or sidebar so she can access it anytime without asking me?

Symptom: Lauren needs a direct link on her POD to access her specific saved exercise program "6-Week Postpartum Core & Resilience Block" without having to navigate through multiple pages.

Likely cause: The exercise block rendering in `render_schedule.py` shows Lauren's daily exercise slots but doesn't include a direct link to her full saved program details. The program exists in the programs system but there's no UI connection from the POD's exercise section to the specific program view.

Risk areas: Adding program-specific links could complicate the POD layout if multiple people have different programs. The link generation might break if it assumes a specific program naming convention or storage format. Changes to the exercise block rendering could affect the overall POD structure or mobile display.

Replit prompt:
In render_schedule.py, locate where Lauren's exercise blocks are rendered in her POD and add a "View Full Program" or "Program Details" link that takes her directly to her saved "6-Week Postpartum Core & Resilience Block" program. The link should appear within or adjacent to her exercise time slots and navigate to the appropriate program view page. Make sure the link is only shown when Lauren has a saved program and style it to integrate naturally with the existing exercise block design. Follow Python 3.11 constraints: no backslashes inside f-strings and keep all imports at the top of the file. Do not change any file other than render_schedule.py unless absolutely necessary. Preserve all existing elif routing structure in do_POST and do_GET — do not convert any elif chain into separate if blocks and do not reorder existing elif branches. After making changes, confirm nothing in do_POST or do_GET was restructured.

Lauren wants a "Print FROL" feature that generates a beautiful, printer-friendly 7-page PDF — one full day per page (Monday through Sunday). Each page should show the complete schedule for all five family members (Mom, JP, Joseph, Michael, James) for that day, formatted cleanly and ready to print or save as PDF. She'd like to access this from the dashboard so she can print on demand.

Symptom: Lauren wants a "Print FROL" feature that generates a 7-page printer-friendly PDF with one complete day per page showing all five family members' schedules for Monday through Sunday.

Likely cause: This new PDF generation functionality needs to be added to the system, likely in a new route in `app.py` that processes the family schedule data and formats it into a printable PDF. The family schedule data exists in the Rule of Life system but there's currently no PDF export capability for the complete weekly schedule.

Risk areas: Adding PDF generation could introduce new dependencies or library imports that might conflict with existing code. The new route could interfere with existing PDF functionality in school_pdf_engine.py if not properly scoped. Large PDF generation might impact server performance or memory usage.

Replit prompt:
Add a new route in app.py (like /print-frol or /frol-pdf) that generates a 7-page PDF of the complete Family Rule of Life schedule. Each page should display one day (Monday through Sunday) with all five family members' schedules (Mom, JP, Joseph, Michael, James) clearly formatted for printing. Use a PDF generation library like reportlab or weasyprint to create clean, readable layouts. Add a "Print FROL" button or link on the main dashboard that connects to this new route. The PDF should be downloadable with a filename like "McAdams_Family_Schedule.pdf". Follow Python 3.11 constraints: no backslashes inside f-strings and keep all imports at the top of the file. Do not change any file other than app.py unless absolutely necessary. Preserve all existing elif routing structure in do_POST and do_GET — do not convert any elif chain into separate if blocks and do not reorder existing elif branches. After making changes, confirm nothing in do_POST or do_GET was restructured.

Lauren is seeing two fitness programs showing up in her calendar and suspects one might be a duplicate or mistake. Can you (1) build her a dedicated Programs page so she can view and manage all saved programs in one place, and (2) help her identify which two programs are appearing on her calendar so we can remove the erroneous one?

Symptom: Lauren sees two fitness programs appearing on her calendar and suspects one is a duplicate or mistake, and she needs a dedicated Programs page to view and manage all saved programs in one place.

Likely cause: The Programs management functionality likely exists in `render_programs.py` but may not have a complete interface for viewing and managing all saved programs. The duplicate programs appearing on the calendar suggests either the calendar rendering logic in `render_calendar.py` is pulling from multiple sources, or there are actually duplicate program entries stored in the data files that need to be identified and cleaned up.

Risk areas: Changes to the programs page could affect existing program storage or retrieval logic. The calendar rendering system might have complex dependencies on program data that could break if we modify how programs are displayed or managed. Multiple data files might be involved in program storage, requiring careful coordination.

Replit prompt:
Enhance render_programs.py to create a comprehensive Programs management page that displays all saved fitness programs for Lauren with options to view details, edit, or delete each program. The page should clearly show program names, creation dates, and current status so duplicates can be easily identified. Add debugging information to help identify which specific programs are being pulled into the calendar display. Ensure the page includes proper navigation and integrates with the existing program storage system. Follow Python 3.11 constraints: no backslashes inside f-strings and keep all imports at the top of the file. Do not change any file other than render_programs.py unless absolutely necessary. Preserve all existing elif routing structure in do_POST and do_GET — do not convert any elif chain into separate if blocks and do not reorder existing elif branches. After making changes, confirm nothing in do_POST or do_GET was restructured.

Lauren is looking for the AI Plan Integrator tool that used to be linked from the homescreen. It's no longer visible after the recent redesign. Can you help her find where it moved to, or if it needs to be re-added to the nav? She needs to access it for task planning.

Symptom: Lauren can't find the AI Plan Integrator tool that used to be linked from the homescreen after the recent redesign and needs to access it for task planning.

Likely cause: The Plan Integrator functionality exists in `render_plan_importer.py` and has a route in `app.py`, but the navigation link was likely removed or relocated during the recent redesign. The tool itself probably still works, but the UI link connecting to it from the main navigation or homescreen is missing from either the homescreen rendering or the top navigation in `ui_helpers.py`.

Risk areas: Adding navigation links could conflict with the current navigation structure or homescreen layout. The Plan Integrator route might have changed or the tool might need updates to work with the current system. Changes to the main navigation could affect the overall site structure or mobile responsiveness.

Replit prompt:
Locate the Plan Integrator route in app.py (likely something like /plan-importer) and verify it's working. Then add a visible "Plan Integrator" or "AI Plan Integrator" link back to the main navigation or homescreen so Lauren can easily access this tool for task planning. Check if the link should go in the top navigation in ui_helpers.py or on the main dashboard/homescreen rendering. Ensure the link is prominently placed where Lauren would expect to find planning tools. Follow Python 3.11 constraints: no backslashes inside f-strings and keep all imports at the top of files. Do not change any file other than app.py and ui_helpers.py unless absolutely necessary. Preserve all existing elif routing structure in do_POST and do_GET — do not convert any elif chain into separate if blocks and do not reorder existing elif branches. After making changes, confirm nothing in do_POST or do_GET was restructured.
